request.question: What is the stage name of the singer who qualified to perform in the final round of Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015, along with  Monika Linkytė and Vaidas Baumila?

title: Vaidas Baumila
Serbia in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015
Mia (singer)
Poland in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015
Monika Linkytė
This Time (Monika Linkytė & Vaidas Baumila song)
Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015
Macedonia in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015
Junior Eurovision Song Contest 2015
Australia in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015
sentences: Vaidas Jacob Baumila (born 28 March 1987 in Vilnius) is a Lithuanian singer and actor.
He represented Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 along with Monika Linkytė with the song "This Time".
Serbia participated in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 with the song "Beauty Never Lies", written by Vladimir Graić and Charlie Mason.
The song was performed by Bojana Stamenov.

Serbian broadcaster Radio Television of Serbia (RTS) selected Vladimir Graić, the composer of the Eurovision Song Contest 2007 winning song "Molitva", to compose three candidate songs as potential Serbian entries for the 2015 contest in Vienna, Austria.

Graić was also tasked with selecting the performers of these three entries, opting to selected two established Serbian artists, Bojana Stamenov and Aleksa Jelić, and one undiscovered talent, Danica Krstić, to perform the three entries.

RTS held the national final "Odbrojavanje za Beč" where the song "Ceo svet je moj" performed by Bojana Stamenov emerged as the winner after topping both the votes of a jury panel and a public televote.
The entry was later translated to English as "Beauty Never Lies" with lyrics written by Charlie Mason—the lyricist of Austria's Eurovision Song Contest 2014 winning song "Rise Like a Phoenix".
Vilija Pilibaitytė (born 19 July 1983), better known by her stage name Mia, is a Lithuanian singer, songwriter, and television host.
Poland participated in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 with the song "In the Name of Love", written by Kuba Raczyński and Monika Kuszyńska.
The song was performed by Monika Kuszyńska, who was selected by Polish broadcaster Telewizja Polska (TVP) to represent the nation at the 2015 contest in Vienna, Austria.
The selection of Kuszyńska along with her song "In the Name of Love" were presented to the public in March 2015.
In the second of the Eurovision semi-finals "In the Name of Love" placed eighth out of the 17 participating countries, securing its place among the 27 other songs in the final.
In Poland's eighteenth Eurovision appearance on 23 May, "In the Name of Love" finished in twenty-third place, receiving 10 points.
Monika Linkytė (born 3 June 1992) is Lithuanian singer who represented Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 along with Vaidas Baumila with the song "This Time".
She also represented Lithuania in the "New Wave 2014".
Linkytė has previously attempted to represent Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest in 2010, 2011, 2012, 2013, 2014 and in the Junior Eurovision Song Contest in 2007.
She was also a finalist on season two of "Lietuvos Balsas".
This Time (Monika Linkytė & Vaidas Baumila song)
Lithuania participated in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 with the song "This Time", written by Vytautas Bikus and Monika Liubinaitė.
The song was performed by Monika Linkytė and Vaidas Baumila.

Lithuanian broadcaster Lithuanian National Radio and Television (LRT) organised the national final ""Eurovizijos" dainų konkurso nacionalinė atranka" in order to select the Lithuanian entry for the 2015 contest in Vienna, Austria.
The national selection involved artists competing independently from their songs with separate selections running concurrently to choose the winning artist and song.
"This Time" was selected as the winning song from a field of twelve competing songs.
Three artists qualified to perform the winning song in the final round of the selection: Mia, Monika Linkytė and Vaidas Baumila.

While Monika Linkytė and Vaidas Baumila entered the competition performing as solo acts, they joined forces as a duet for the final artist selection and were subsequently selected as the performers for "This Time".
Macedonia participated in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 with the song "Autumn Leaves", written by Joacim Persson.
The song was performed by Daniel Kajmakoski.

In November 2014, Macedonian broadcaster Macedonian Radio Television (MRT) organised the "Skopje Festival 2014" in order to select the Macedonian entry for the 2015 Eurovision Song Contest in Vienna, Austria.
Daniel Kajmakoski and the song "Lisja esenski" emerged as the winner.
The song competed in Macedonia's national final in Macedonian with lyrics by Aleksandar Mitevski and Kajmakoski himself.
At the Eurovision Song Contest, however, Kajmakoski opted to perform the song in English under the title "Autumn Leaves".
In the first of the Eurovision semi-finals, Macedonia failed to qualify to the final, placing fifteenth out of the 16 participating countries with 28 points.
The Junior Eurovision Song Contest 2015 was the thirteenth annual Junior Eurovision Song Contest, and took place, for the first time, in Bulgaria.
The Bulgarian national broadcaster BNT was the host broadcaster for the event.
The final took place on 21 November 2015 and was held at the Arena Armeec in Sofia.
Poli Genova, a Bulgarian singer and former representative of Bulgaria in the Eurovision Song Contest 2011 and the Eurovision Song Contest 2016, hosted the show.
A total of seventeen countries participated, with Australia and Ireland making their debuts.
Albania and Macedonia returned after being absent since the 2012 and 2013 contests, respectively.
Croatia and Cyprus withdrew after returning in the 2014 edition , while Sweden withdrew for the first time since 2008.
Australia participated in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 in Vienna, Austria.
Owing to its cultural and political ties to European countries, the Eurovision Song Contest has been televised in Australia by the public broadcaster SBS for over thirty years.

In honour of the contest's 60th running and in recognition of the event's popularity in Australia, it was announced on 10 February 2015 that Australia was to be invited to compete in the 2015 contest as a special guest participant.
Australians have participated in and even won Eurovision as representatives of other countries.

In 2015 for example, the Australian coloratura soprano of Armenian descent Mary-Jean O'Doherty participated in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 contest as part of the group Genealogy representing Armenia.
But this marked the first time that the country has officially participated.

title: Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015
Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015
Mia (singer)

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
1) Evidence found:
- "Mia, Monika Linkytė and Vaidas Baumila" (quote: "Mia, Monika Linkytė and Vaidas Baumila") — passage 1.
- Monika Linkytė: "Monika Linkytė (born 3 June 1992)" — passage 2.
- Vaidas Baumila: "Vaidas Jacob Baumila (born 28 March 1987)" — passage 3.

2) Ambiguities:
- The name "Mia" appears only as "Mia" in passage 1; no dedicated page/title for "Mia" is present in the supplied passages. There may be multiple artists known as "Mia" (not disambiguated here).

3) Bridge entity / Missing fact:
- Bridge entity: "Mia" (as listed in passage 1).
- Missing fact needed: the singer's stage name (i.e., the canonical stage name or the artist’s full/real name corresponding to the "Mia" who qualified) — this exact fact is not present in the provided passages.

4) Retrieval direction (prioritized):
- 1) Open a page titled "Mia (singer)" or "Mia (Lithuanian singer)" — check the lede/infobox and Biography/Career for stage name, legal/full name, and mention of the 2015 national final qualification. Search keywords: Mia Lithuanian singer 2015 stage name; "Mia Eurovizijos dainų konkurso nacionalinė atranka".
- 2) Fetch the national selection page "Eurovizijos dainų konkurso nacionalinė atranka" (or the full "Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015" article) and check the section listing final-round qualifiers for links to Mia's page or an explanatory note about her name.
- 3) If needed, open "Mia (disambiguation)" to identify candidate Mia pages and then check their ledes/infoboxes for the correct Lithuanian artist.

Do not provide the final answer yet — retrieve the above pages/sections to confirm the singer's official stage name.

Lithuania in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 | Lithuania participated in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 with the song "This Time", written by Vytautas Bikus and Monika Liubinaitė.  The song was performed by Monika Linkytė and Vaidas Baumila.  Lithuanian broadcaster Lithuanian National Radio and Television (LRT) organised the national final ""Eurovizijos" dainų konkurso nacionalinė atranka" in order to select the Lithuanian entry for the 2015 contest in Vienna, Austria.  The national selection involved artists competing independently from their songs with separate selections running concurrently to choose the winning artist and song.  "This Time" was selected as the winning song from a field of twelve competing songs.  Three artists qualified to perform the winning song in the final round of the selection: Mia, Monika Linkytė and Vaidas Baumila.  While Monika Linkytė and Vaidas Baumila entered the competition performing as solo acts, they joined forces as a duet for the final artist selection and were subsequently selected as the performers for "This Time".

Samanta Tīna | Samanta Poļakova (born 31 March 1989), better known by her stage name Samanta Tīna is a Latvian singer.  She has attempted to represent Latvia in the Eurovision Song Contest four times, in 2012, 2013, 2014, and 2016 and Lithuania in 2017, and also has taken part in the Lithuanian version of "The Voice".

Donny Montell | Donatas Montvydas (born 22 October 1987), better known by his stage name Donny Montell, is a Lithuanian singer-songwriter who represented Lithuania at the Eurovision Song Contest 2012, held in Baku, Azerbaijan.  He did so for a second time in 2016, when he represented his country in Stockholm, Sweden.

Love (Sasha Son song) | "Love", known by its Lithuanian name "Pasiklydęs žmogus" ("The Lost Man") is a song by Lithuanian singer Sasha Son, the Lithuanian entry to the Eurovision Song Contest 2009, where it was performed in English with the final verse in Russian.

Måns Zelmerlöw | Måns Petter Albert Sahlén Zelmerlöw (] ; born 13 June 1986) is a Swedish pop singer and television presenter, winner in 2015 of the Eurovision Song Contest.  He took part in "Idol 2005", eventually finishing fifth, won the first season of "Let's Dance," and scored a hit with his 2007 song "Cara Mia", which was his entry in that year's Melodifestivalen.  Zelmerlöw was the host of "Allsång på Skansen" from 2011 to 2013.  He participated in Melodifestivalen in 2007, 2009 and won in 2015.  Zelmerlöw represented Sweden in the Eurovision Song Contest 2015 with the song "Heroes", winning the contest with 365 points.  Zelmerlöw and Petra Mede co-hosted the Eurovision Song Contest 2016 held in Stockholm.
La mia poca grande età | La mia poca grande età is the debut album by Italian singer-songwriter Alice, released under the stage name "Alice Visconti" in 1975 on CBS Records.
